upstream.source: field-research (Meridian): works/2026-07-01-calibration-gap (Instrument 001, Calibration Certificate) + memory/claims.md rows on detector bias, harm cases, and the legal record
upstream.instrument_001_live_status: SHIPPED and live at extraction time, with its 2026-07-03 correction and re-verification history displayed on the work itself; if upstream revises or discards, this work updates on the same cycle or pauses
upstream.contract: Live status travels; load-bearing caveats survive re-voicing; corrections flow upstream, never silently sideways

naming_policy.statement: No personal names in this work's own voice — headlines, dramaturgy, verdicts. People appear as role + institution + consequence. Personal names survive only as citation text inside linked sources (official case captions, bylines), where they are evidence, not spectacle. Companies and tools are named where every claim is traceable to a cited primary source.

figures.note: Direct fields (session 09, discharging Kritiker note 4): the work renders these numbers from here, never parsed out of prose — a wording change can no longer silently break the central figure

liang_2023.claim: AI text detectors flag non-native-English-speaker writing as AI-generated at far higher rates than native writing: on 91 TOEFL essays, 89 (97.8%) were flagged by at least one of the seven detectors studied; the average false-positive rate across the seven detectors was 61.22%; 18 of 91 (19.8%) were flagged unanimously; native-speaker control essays were near-zero
liang_2023.mechanism: Perplexity-based detection penalizes constrained vocabulary — the careful, limited word choice of a second-language writer reads as machine-like regularity
liang_2023.source: Liang, Yuksekgonul, Mao, Wu, Zou — Cell Patterns 2023
discipline: 97.8% is the union statistic (flagged by ≥1 of 7 detectors) — never attribute it to a single detector
61.22% is the mean FPR across the seven — per-detector rates were not individually retrieved upstream; do not attribute the average to a specific tool
Population effect, not an individual guarantee: one careful sentence is not guaranteed to be flagged

case_institutional.claim: Australian Catholic University, 2024: roughly 6,000 academic-misconduct allegations (around 90% AI-related); about 25% were dismissed; a Deputy Vice-Chancellor called the figures 'substantially overstated'; the university abandoned Turnitin AI detection in March 2025

case_yale.claim: An EMBA student at Yale School of Management, a French national writing in his second language: one-year suspension and a failing grade after a GPTZero flag on a final exam. He submitted scans showing the same tool flagged academic papers by Yale scholars, including a former university president, as probably AI-generated. His federal lawsuit is PENDING (D. Conn. 3:25-cv-00159); a preliminary injunction was denied in May 2025
case_yale.discipline: PENDING — never state as decided. Named-individuals policy applies: the person appears here as role + institution + consequence; the official case caption is citation text only
case_yale.caption: Rignol v. Yale (pending)

case_minnesota_boundary.role: BOUNDARY CASE — DELIBERATELY NOT CARRIED ON THE VERDICT CARD
case_minnesota_boundary.claim: The most widely reported consequence case — a PhD student expelled from the University of Minnesota over alleged AI use, student visa revoked, expulsion upheld by the Minnesota Court of Appeals in February 2026, federal lawsuit dismissed in October 2025 — is NOT presented on this work as detector harm. The appeals court noted the disciplinary panel did not rely on AI-detection evidence: it credited graders' ability to identify AI-written work, pointed to irrelevant sources, and cited missing citations and inconsistent testimony. A work about machine judgment may not borrow stakes the record does not attribute to the machine
case_minnesota_boundary.caption: Yang v. University of Minnesota (Minn. Ct. App., Feb 2026)
case_minnesota_boundary.note: Reported upstream to the research wing on 2026-07-12 (their instrument 001 lists this case in its harm register): the appellate framing is a load-bearing caveat there

legal_void.claim_frame: The nearby law governs something else: the EU AI Act's Article 50 obliges PROVIDERS to mark machine OUTPUT as machine-made. In the cases retrieved here, no court reviewed the machine's judgment of a human's words itself — where consequences were challenged, review ran on whether the institution followed its own procedures, not on whether the detector was right
upstream_basis: Claims ledger row on the harm cases: 'courts found institutions followed their own procedures even where the underlying tool has known false-positive rates'

Claims ledger row on the England & Wales ombudsman record (searched-and-not-found, scoped): detector output carries no evidentiary presumption of misconduct; the burden of proof stays on the provider; the pure 'detector output alone as dispositive' scenario was never squarely adjudicated in the cases reviewed. This is a procedural-fairness finding from a non-binding ombudsman in one jurisdiction — not a proven universal negative

The most-cited detection vendor's own statement: 'we provide data for educators to make an informed decision ... Turnitin does not make a determination of misconduct even in the space of text similarity'
legal_void.discipline: State as searched-and-scoped, never as a proven universal negative. Do not conflate with, or re-grade, upstream instrument 011's card 001 (live status there: UNSETTLED-but-informed)

counter_evidence.role: LOAD-BEARING CAVEAT — survives every re-voicing of this work
claims: A 2026 study in a Czech-language setting found NO systematic bias against non-native speakers; the contemporary detectors studied did not rely on perplexity as a key feature (Al Ali, Helcl, Libovický, EACL 2026). The bias may be perplexity-architecture-specific and/or English-specific, or diminishing in newer models — not resolved

Turnitin's own research (vendor, on its own tool) reports no statistically significant FPR bias against English-language learners at 300+ words — and Liang et al.'s seven detectors did not include Turnitin. Short documents show a significantly larger false-positive gap
The documented pattern is real and sourced — and it is documented, not universal

short_text.claim: Turnitin's own research: 'too short' documents show a significantly larger false-positive gap; its document-level FPR claim of <1% holds only for documents with 20%+ AI writing

meter.statement: The meter on this page is NOT GPTZero, Turnitin, or any commercial detector. It is a deterministic, in-browser reconstruction of the documented mechanism — perplexity-and-burstiness-style scoring — built by this studio so that the mechanism can be experienced and checked. Same input, same verdict, always; the full scoring function and its lexicon are in this page's source; the score breakdown is shown for every sample. Its verdict proves nothing about you. The verified figures above prove the pattern at population scale.
components: COMMONNESS (perplexity proxy): the share of your words drawn from a built-in list of very common English words — constrained, careful vocabulary scores machine-ward, exactly the documented mechanism
BURSTINESS: variation in sentence and word length — even, careful rhythm scores machine-ward
IRREGULARITY: contractions, informal tokens, loose punctuation — their absence scores machine-ward
DIVERSITY: type-token ratio — a narrow working vocabulary scores machine-ward
REPETITION: repeated word pairs score machine-ward
meter.attempt_blind: The scoring function receives only the text. It cannot see which attempt it is scoring. A scripted flip would be a lie; this meter cannot tell your first sentence from your second.

imagined.elements: The border-checkpoint dramaturgy, the gate's voice, the prompts, the stamps, and the self-test sentences are fiction by this studio — marked as such on the work's face. The figures and consequences inside the verdict card are not.

tally.scope: This terminal only (this browser): counted in local storage, never transmitted. The aggregate argument is carried by the verified population figures, not by this tally.
tally.non_flip_disclosure: Your careful sentence may pass. Liang et al. is a population effect, not a per-person guarantee: 89 of 91 flagged means 2 walked through. An ADMITTED second attempt does not acquit the tool — it makes you one of the two.
